planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; climbing bleeding heart is a biennial that germinates in late spring, forms a basal rosette in its first year, then climbs and blooms in its second year. It self-sows freely in moist, shaded conditions and can be treated as a self-perpetuating annual colony once established.

directions: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full shade at the base of a trellis, fence, or scrambling support; climbing bleeding heart is a unique native biennial vine of moist, rocky, shaded slopes and forest margins in the Appalachians and northeastern North America. The lacy, glaucous blue-green foliage is extremely finely divided and feathery, resembling the leaves of bleeding heart (Lamprocapnos/Dicentra) or corydalis, and the plant climbs by means of clinging petioles to reach 6–10 feet. The small, nodding, tubular flowers — white to pale pink — are produced in summer of the second year in clusters from the leaf axils, resembling tiny bleeding hearts. Self-sows freely in appropriate conditions to form a naturalized colony. An unusual and beautiful native vine for shaded woodland trellises and shrub edges, little known in cultivation.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or very early spring in well-drained, rocky, sandy, or disturbed lean soil in partial shade to full sun; golden corydalis is a winter annual to biennial that germinates in cool weather and blooms in spring through early summer. It self-sows readily in rocky, open, disturbed areas.

directions: Direct sow in fall in well-drained, rocky, sandy, or lean disturbed soil in partial shade to full sun; golden corydalis is a charming, finely dissected-leaved native annual of rocky, disturbed soils, stream gravel bars, talus slopes, and sandy open ground across a wide range of North America from Alaska to Texas. The bright, pure golden-yellow flowers are produced in dense terminal clusters on sprawling, succulent stems from spring through early summer. Common names 'scrambled eggs' aptly describes the tumbled, bright yellow flower clusters. Self-sows freely in rocky, open, disturbed garden areas to create naturalized colonies that persist indefinitely. One of the more widespread and adaptable native Corydalis species, tolerating a range of climates from boreal to semi-arid. Visited by bumblebees and long-tongued specialist bees that access the spurred flowers.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or sow fresh seed in cracks and crevices in stone walls, pavement gaps, or rocky soil in partial shade to full shade; yellow corydalis self-sows prolifically and naturalizes aggressively in mortar joints, wall crevices, and shaded rocky areas. No soil preparation is needed — just sow where you want it to grow.

directions: Sow fresh seeds directly into wall crevices, gaps in paving, or rocky, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; yellow corydalis (now Pseudofumaria lutea, formerly Corydalis lutea) is a European perennial that has naturalized widely in shaded stone walls, garden walls, rocky areas, and cracks in pavement throughout the temperate world. The finely divided, blue-green, fern-like foliage is evergreen in mild climates and produces cheerful sprays of bright yellow, spurred flowers almost continuously from spring through fall. It is one of the most effective self-sowing plants for naturalizing in shaded wall crevices, rock garden pockets, and the base of shaded stone retaining walls, where it thrives in the excellent drainage and cool moisture that lime mortar provides. Self-sows so prolifically that a single plant can colonize an entire wall within a few years. Beloved in cottage garden and woodland garden traditions.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; tall white lettuce grows naturally in moist, shaded deciduous forests and forest margins throughout eastern North America. It tolerates deep shade better than most tall composite-family plants.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; tall white lettuce is one of the tallest composite-family plants adapted to true forest shade, growing naturally in the understory of moist deciduous forests where it can reach 9 feet. The large, deeply lobed, variable leaves resemble those of garden lettuce — the plant is indeed related to Lactuca — and produce a milky sap when broken. The small, creamy-white, nodding flower heads in late summer and fall are subtly beautiful and attract specialist native bees. The fluffy seed heads provide food for goldfinches and other seed-eating birds in fall. The plant's ability to grow in deep forest shade makes it one of the few tall, imposing plants appropriate for heavily shaded woodland gardens where most large plants fail. Self-sows readily in appropriate conditions.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic, rocky or sandy soil in partial to full shade; small-flowered alumroot is a limestone cliff and cave-entrance specialist native to the Appalachians, preferring the cool, consistently moist, very well-drained conditions of shaded rock overhangs and cliff faces.

directions: Plant in moist, very well-drained, humus-rich, rocky, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; small-flowered alumroot is an Appalachian native of limestone cliffs, cave entrances, and shaded rocky outcrops, naturally growing in the perpetually cool, moist, well-drained environment of rock overhangs where moisture seeps through rather than pooling. The rounded, maple-like, dark green leaves form low, neat rosettes and are attractive year-round as a semi-evergreen groundcover in appropriate rocky, shaded garden settings. The tiny, white flowers on airy, branching panicles in late summer are less showy than garden heuchera cultivars but provide nectar for small native bees. An excellent choice for shaded rock gardens, crevices in stone walls, and the base of large boulders in woodland gardens in zones 4–8.
